title: Topological Anderson insulators and reentrant topological transitions in a quasiperiodic long-range Su-Schrieffer-Heeger model
abstract: We study a one-dimensional long-range Su-Schrieffer-Heeger model with third-nearest-neighbor hopping and subject to quasiperiodic disorder. In the clean limit, the model hosts phases characterized by winding numbers $W=-1,0,1$ and $2$. The introduction of quasiperiodic disorder profoundly modifies the phase diagram and induces a series of topological phase transitions. Owing to the competition between topological dimerization and localization, topological Anderson insulating (TAI) phases with different winding numbers emerge and can persist even when the spectral gap becomes nearly closed in the strong-disorder regime. In addition, we uncover multiple reentrant topological phase transitions induced by varying either the quasiperiodic disorder strength or the hopping amplitudes. Remarkably, the system exhibits staircase-like topological Anderson transitions, where the real-space winding number evolves through successive quantized steps with increasing disorder strength. Our results demonstrate that the interplay between long-range hopping and quasiperiodic disorder generates a rich landscape of disorder-induced topological phases and reentrant topological transition phenomena.
summary: This paper theoretically explores the complex topology of a 1D SSH model subjected to quasiperiodic disorder. It demonstrates that this combination leads to Topological Anderson Insulators (TAIs) characterized by multiple winding numbers and exhibits rich reentrant phase transitions. The findings highlight how disorder can induce quantized, staircase-like changes in topological invariants.
main_problem: The study investigates topological Anderson insulators (TAIs) and complex reentrant topological phase transitions within a quasiperiodic long-range Su-Schrieffer-Heeger model.
main_result: The interplay between long-range hopping and quasiperiodic disorder generates a rich landscape featuring TAI phases with multiple winding numbers, alongside observable staircase-like and reentrant topological transitions.
method: Analysis relies on calculating the real-space winding number (a topological invariant) and monitoring the Lyapunov exponent ($\gamma_{LE}$) to characterize phase boundaries in both clean and disordered limits.
model_or_system: The system is a one-dimensional long-range Su-Schrieffer-Heeger model incorporating third-nearest-neighbor hopping, subjected to quasiperiodic disorder applied to its hopping amplitudes. The Hamiltonian structure allows for the emergence of multiple topological invariants ($W=-1, 0, 1, 2$).
key_observables: Real-space winding number (W), Lyapunov exponent ($\gamma_{LE}$), and bulk energy gap ($\Delta E$).
important_parameters: Disorder strength ($\lambda$), quasiperiodic modulation parameter ($\alpha$), and various hopping amplitudes ($t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4$).
main_assumptions: All hopping amplitudes are assumed to be real; the analysis uses the transfer-matrix formalism.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate phase diagrams showing regions for different winding numbers in the clean limit (Fig. 1), and demonstrate reentrant transitions, staircase changes, and spectral gap evolution as disorder strength ($\lambda$) is varied (Figs. 3, 4, 5, 6).
paper_structure: The paper systematically introduces the model and characterization methods, analyzes the clean limit topology, investigates TAI phases under quasiperiodic disorder, and concludes by summarizing the rich landscape of disorder-induced topological phenomena.
why_interesting: The study provides a concrete theoretical framework for understanding how long-range interactions combined with incommensurate potentials (quasiperiodicity) can stabilize exotic topological states that are robust even when spectral gaps close, which is highly relevant to engineered quantum materials and lattice simulations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (Color online) Phase diagram of the clean long-range SSH model in the t3-t4 plane for t1 = 1 and t2 = 1.1. The colored regions correspond to phases with winding numbers W = −1, 0, 1, and 2. The solid lines denote the phase bound- aries determined from the bulk-gap closing conditions.

FIG. 2. (Color online) Schematic illustration of the dimeriza- tion configurations and the associated zero-energy edge states in the long-range SSH model. The blue and orange circles rep- resent the two sublattices of each unit cell, while the red lines denote the dominant hopping amplitudes. Panels (a)–(d) cor- respond to the phases with winding numbers W = 0, 1, −1, and 2, respectively. The number and sublattice character of the unpaired boundary sites determine the topological invari- ant and the corresponding zero-energy edge modes.

FIG. 4. (Color online) Topological phase diagrams in the t1-λ plane for the long-range SSH model with quasiperiodic disorder in the intracell hopping t1. Different colors denote phases with different winding numbers. (a) Phase diagram for t2 = 0.2, t3 = 0.8, and t4 = 1.1. The black dashed line marks the cut at t1 = 0.55, while the white dashed line corresponds to λ = 2.3, which exhibits multiple reentrant transitions in the system. (b) Phase diagram for t2 = 0.7, t3 = 1.1, and t4 = 0.9. The black and white dashed lines indicate the cuts at t1 = 0.1 and λ = 1.5, respectively, along which additional reentrant topological transitions occur.

FIG. 3. (Color online) Topological phases of the long-range SSH model with quasiperiodic disorder in the intracell hop- ping t1. (a) Energy spectrum as a function of the disorder strength λ under open boundary conditions. The red line rep- resents the zero-energy edge modes. (b) Spatial distributions of the four zero-energy edge modes at λ = 0.6. (c) Lyapunov exponent of the zero-energy modes γLE (black dotted line) and the bulk gap ∆E around zero energy (red solid line) as functions of λ. The inset enlarges the region enclosed by the red dashed ellipse. (d) Logarithm of the bulk gap, log(∆E), as a function of system size for λ = 4.06 (red dot-dashed line) and λ = 4.88 (red solid line). (e)...

Figure 4 presents two additional phase diagrams in the t1-λ plane for different parameter sets, revealing even richer disorder-induced topological phenomena. Along the black dashed line at t1 = 0.55 in Fig. 4(a), the wind- ing number evolves according to W = 2 →1 →2 → 1 →0 as the disorder strength increases. This sequence demonstrates reentrant topological transitions between the W = 2 and W = 1 phases. Reentrant behavior can also be induced by varying t1. Along the white dashed line in Fig. 4(a), the system undergoes repeated transi- tions between the W = 1 and W = 0 phases, providing another example of multiple reentrant topological tran- sitions.
